title: Definite integrals of piecewise functions
description: Evaluate definite integrals of piecewise functions.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and indefinite integrals: basic rules and notation: definite integrals

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
x +1 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\lt0 \\
\cos(\pi x) &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \geq0\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}(x + 1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}\cos(\pi x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}\big(x + 1\big)\,dx~ &=\dfrac12x^2 + x\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12\cdot (\blueD 0)^2 + (\blueD0 )\right] - \left[\dfrac12\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^2 + (\maroonD{-1}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-\dfrac12 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac12}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}\cos(\pi x)\,dx~ &=\dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac{\sin(\pi \cdot \blueD 1)}{\pi}\right] - \left[\dfrac{\sin(\pi \cdot \maroonD 0)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac{\sin(\pi)}{\pi}\right] - \left[\dfrac{\sin(0)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&= [0] - [0] \\\\
&= \greenD{0}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}(x + 1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}\cos(\pi x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{\dfrac12} + \greenD{0}$

$ = \dfrac12$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx = \dfrac12$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
-2x +6 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\lt2 \\
2\cos(\pi x) &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \geq2\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^2_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^4_{2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^2_{1}(-2x +6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^4_{2}2\cos(\pi x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^2_{1} (-2x +6)\,dx~ &=-x^2 + 6x\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[-(\blueD 2)^2 + 6\cdot(\blueD2) \right] - \left[-(\maroonD{1})^2 + 6\cdot(\maroonD{1}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[8\right] -\left[5 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{3}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{2}2\cos(\pi x)\,dx~ &=\dfrac{2\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{2}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac{2\sin(\pi \cdot \blueD 4)}{\pi}\right] - \left[\dfrac{2\sin(\pi \cdot \maroonD 2)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac{2\sin(4\pi)}{\pi}\right] - \left[\dfrac{2\sin(2\pi)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&= [0] - [0] \\\\
&= \greenD{0}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^2_{1}(-2x +6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^4_{2}2\cos(\pi x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{3} + \greenD{0}$

$ = 3$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx = 3$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
-6x+6 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\lt1 \\
\sin(\pi x) &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \geq1\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{1}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(-6x+6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\sin(\pi x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align}  \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(-6x+6)\,dx~ &=-3x^2 + 6x\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[-3 (\blueD 1)^2 + 6\cdot(\blueD1) \right] - \left[-3(\maroonD{0})^2 +  6\cdot(\maroonD{0}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[3\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{3}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\sin(\pi x)\,dx~ &=-\dfrac{\cos(\pi x)}{\pi}\Bigg|^\blueD3_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[-\dfrac{\cos(\pi \cdot \blueD 3)}{\pi}\right] - \left[-\dfrac{\cos(\pi \cdot \maroonD 1)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&= \left[-\dfrac{\cos(3\pi)}{\pi}\right] - \left[-\dfrac{\cos(\pi)}{\pi}\right]  \\\\
&=\left [\dfrac1\pi\right] - \left [\dfrac1\pi\right] \\\\
&= \greenD{0}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(-6x+6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\sin(\pi x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{3} + \greenD{0}$

$ = 3$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = 3$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-\frac{\pi}2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-\frac{\pi}2}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-\frac{\pi}2}\cos(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1-2x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-\frac{\pi}2}\cos(x)\,dx~ &=\sin(x)\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-\frac{\pi}2}} \\\\
&= \left[\sin( \blueD 0)\right] - \left[\sin\left( \maroonD {-\dfrac\pi2}\right)\right]   \\\\
&=[0] - [-1]  \\\\
&= \greenD{1}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1-2x)\,dx~ &=x-x^2\Bigg|^\blueD{1}_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[(\blueD1)- (\blueD 1)^2  \right] - \left[ (\maroonD{0}) - (\maroonD{0})^2 \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{0}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-\frac{\pi}2}\cos(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1-2x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{1} + \greenD{0}$

$ = 1$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-\frac{\pi}2}f(x)\,dx = 1$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^\pi_{-\pi}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^\frac\pi2_{-\pi}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^\pi_{\frac\pi2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^\frac\pi2_{-\pi}\sin(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^\pi_{\frac\pi2}\dfrac{2x}\pi\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^\frac\pi2_{-\pi}\sin(x)\,dx~ &=-\cos(x)\Bigg|^\blueD{\frac\pi2}_{\maroonD{-\pi}} \\\\
&= \left[-\cos\left(\blueD {\dfrac\pi2}\right)\right] - \left[-\cos(\maroonD{-\pi})\right]  \\\\
&= \left[0\right] - \left[1\right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-1}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align}  \displaystyle\int^\pi_{\frac\pi2}\dfrac{2x}\pi\,dx~ &=\dfrac{x^2}\pi\Bigg|^\blueD{\pi}_{\maroonD{\frac\pi2}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac{\left( \blueD{\pi} \right)^2}{\pi}\right] - \left[\dfrac{\left( \maroonD{\dfrac\pi2} \right)^2}{\pi}\right]   \\\\
 &= \left[\pi\right] -\left[\dfrac\pi4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{3\pi}4}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^\frac\pi2_{-\pi}\sin(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^\pi_{\frac\pi2}\dfrac{2x}\pi\,dx$

$ = \greenD{-1} + \greenD{\dfrac{3\pi}4}$

$ = \dfrac{3\pi}4-1$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^\pi_{-\pi}f(x)\,dx =\dfrac{3\pi}4-1$

##Splitting up the absolute value 

Notice that the absolute value function is a piecewise function. Here we have that: 

$f(x) = \begin{cases}
x -1 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\geq1 \\
1-x &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \lt1\end{cases}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^3_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^3_{1}(x - 1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1-x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^3_{1}(x - 1)\,dx~ &=\dfrac12x^2 - x\Bigg|^\blueD3_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12 (\blueD 3)^2 - (\blueD3) \right] - \left[\dfrac12(\maroonD{1})^2 - (\maroonD{1}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac{3}{2}\right] -\left[-\dfrac12 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{2}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} 
\displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1 - x)\,dx~
 &=x - \dfrac12x^2\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[(\blueD1)-\dfrac12 (\blueD 1)^2 \right] - \left[ (\maroonD{0})-\dfrac12(\maroonD{0})^2 \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac{1}{2}\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac12}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{1}(x - 1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(1-x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{2} + \greenD{\dfrac12}$

$ = \dfrac{5}2$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = \dfrac{5}2$

##Splitting up the absolute value 

Notice that the absolute value function is a piecewise function. Here we have that: 

$f(x) = \begin{cases}
x +2 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\geq-2 \\
-2-x &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \lt-2\end{cases}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-4}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-4}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}(x +2)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-4}(-2-x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}(x +2)\,dx~ &=\dfrac12x^2 + 2x\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12 (\blueD 0)^2 + 2\cdot(\blueD0) \right] - \left[\dfrac12 (\maroonD {-2})^2 + 2\cdot(\maroonD{-2}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-2 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{2}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} 
\displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-4}(-2-x)\,dx~
 &=-2x - \dfrac12x^2\Bigg|^\blueD{-2}_{\maroonD{-4}} \\\\
&= \left[- 2\cdot(\blueD{-2}) - \dfrac12\cdot (\blueD {-2})^2 \right] - \left[-2\cdot(\maroonD{-4}) - \dfrac12\cdot (\maroonD {-4})^2 \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[2\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{2}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}(x +2)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-2}_{-4}(-2-x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{2} + \greenD{2}$

$ = 4$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^0_{-4}f(x)\,dx = 4$

##Splitting up the absolute value 

Notice that the absolute value function is a piecewise function. Here we have that: 

$f(x) = \begin{cases}
3x+3 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\geq-1 \\
-3-3x &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \lt-1\end{cases}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(3x + 3)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}(-3-3x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(3x + 3)\,dx~ &=\dfrac32x^2 + 3x\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac32 \cdot(\blueD 1)^2 + 3\cdot(\blueD1) \right] - \left[\dfrac32\cdot(\maroonD{-1})^2 + 3\cdot(\maroonD{-1}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac{9}{2}\right] -\left[-\dfrac32 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{6}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} 
\displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}(-3-3x)\,dx~
 &=-3x - \dfrac32x^2\Bigg|^\blueD{-1}_{\maroonD{-3}} \\\\
&= \left[- 3\cdot(\blueD{-1})-\dfrac32 \cdot(\blueD {-1})^2  \right] - \left[- 3\cdot(\maroonD{-3})-\dfrac32\cdot(\maroonD{-3})^2  \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac{3}{2}\right] -\left[-\dfrac{9}2 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{6}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(3x + 3)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}(-3-3x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{6} + \greenD{6}$

$ = 12$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx = 12$

##Splitting up the absolute value 

Notice that the absolute value function is a piecewise function. Here we have that: 

$f(x) = \begin{cases}
2x -6 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\geq3 \\
6-2x &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \lt3\end{cases}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{3}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x - 6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{0}(6-2x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x - 6)\,dx~ &=x^2 - 6x\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{3}} \\\\
&= \left[ (\blueD 4)^2 - 6\cdot(\blueD4) \right] - \left[(\maroonD{3})^2 - 6\cdot (\maroonD{3}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-8\right] -\left[-9 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{1}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} 
\displaystyle\int^3_{0}(6-2x)\,dx~
 &=6x-x^2\Bigg|^\blueD3_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[6\cdot(\blueD3) - (\blueD 3)^2 \right] - \left[ 6\cdot(\maroonD{0})-(\maroonD{0})^2 \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[9\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{9}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x - 6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^3_{0}(6-2x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{1} + \greenD{9}$

$ = 10$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = 10$

##Splitting up the absolute value 

Notice that the absolute value function is a piecewise function. Here we have that: 

$f(x) = \begin{cases}
2x-4 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\geq2 \\
-2x+4 &  \text{for} ~~~~ x \lt2\end{cases}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{-2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{2}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^2_{-2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{2}(2x - 4)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^2_{-2}(-2x+4)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{2}(2x - 4)\,dx~ &=x^2 - 4x\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{2}} \\\\
&= \left[(\blueD 4)^2 -4\cdot(\blueD4) \right] - \left[(\maroonD{2})^2 - 4\cdot(\maroonD{2}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{4}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} 
\displaystyle\int^2_{-2}(-2x+4)\,dx~
 &=-x^2+4x\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[-(\blueD 2)^2 +4\cdot(\blueD{2})\right] - \left[-( \maroonD{-2})^2+4(\maroonD{-2}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[4\right] -\left[-12 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{16}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{2}(2x - 4)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^2_{-2}(-2x+4)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{4} + \greenD{16}$

$ = 20$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^4_{-2}f(x)\,dx = 20$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^9_{-2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^9_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^9_{0}\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}-x\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^9_{0}x^\frac12\,dx
&=\dfrac23x^\frac32\Bigg|^\blueD9_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac23 (\blueD 9)^\frac32 \right] - \left[\dfrac23 (\maroonD{0})^\frac32\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[18\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{18}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}-x\,dx
&=-\dfrac12x^2\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[-\dfrac12 (\blueD 0)^2 \right] - \left[-\dfrac12 (\maroonD{-2})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-2 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{2}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^9_{0}\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^0_{-2}-x\,dx$

$ = \greenD{18} + \greenD{2}$

$ = 20$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^9_{-2}f(x)\,dx = 20$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{-2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{-2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{1}6\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{-2}(2x+4)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}6\sqrt{x}\,dx
&=4x^\frac32\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[4\cdot (\blueD 4)^\frac32 \right] - \left[4\cdot (\maroonD{1})^\frac32\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[32\right] -\left[4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{28}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align}  \displaystyle\int^1_{-2}(2x+4)\,dx
&=\left(x^2+4x\right)\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[(\blueD 1)^2 +4\cdot(\blueD1)\right] - \left[(\maroonD {-2})^2 +4\cdot(\maroonD{-2})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[5\right] -\left[-4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{9}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}6\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{-2}(2x+4)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{28} + \greenD{9}$

$ = 37$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^4_{-2}f(x)\,dx = 37$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-3}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}9\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^0_{-3}-2x\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}9\sqrt{x}\,dx
&=6x^\frac32\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[6 (\blueD 1)^\frac32 \right] - \left[6 (\maroonD{0})^\frac32\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[6\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{6}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-3}-2x\,dx
&=-x^2\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-3}} \\\\
&= \left[- (\blueD 0)^2 \right] - \left[- (\maroonD{-3})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-9 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{9}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}9\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^0_{-3}-2x\,dx$

$ = \greenD{6} + \greenD{9}$

$ = 15$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx = 15$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^9_{3}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^9_{4}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{4}_{3}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^9_{4}3\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x-8)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^9_{4}3\sqrt{x}\,dx
&=2x^\frac32\Bigg|^\blueD9_{\maroonD{4}} \\\\
&= \left[2 (\blueD 9)^\frac32 \right] - \left[2 (\maroonD{4})^\frac32\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[54\right] -\left[16 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{38}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x-8)\,dx
&=(x^2-8x)\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{3}} \\\\
&= \left[ (\blueD 4)^2 - 8\cdot(\blueD4)\right] - \left[(\maroonD 3)^2 - 8\cdot(\maroonD3)\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-16\right] -\left[-15 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-1}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^9_{4}3\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^4_{3}(2x-8)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{38} + (\greenD{-1})$

$ = 37$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^9_{3}f(x)\,dx = 37$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
-3\sqrt{x } &   \text{for} ~~~~x\gt{1} \\
x-3&  \text{for} ~~~~ x \leq1\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^4_{-4}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{-4}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{-4}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{1}-3\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{-4}(x-3)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}-3\sqrt{x}\,dx
&=-2x^\frac32\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[-2 (\blueD 4)^\frac32 \right] - \left[-2 (\maroonD{1})^\frac32\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-16\right] -\left[-2 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-14}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{-4}(x-3)\,dx
&=\left(\dfrac12x^2-3x\right)\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{-4}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12 \cdot(\blueD 1)^2 -3\cdot(\blueD1) \right] - \left[\dfrac12 \cdot(\maroonD {-4})^2 -3\cdot(\maroonD{-4})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-\dfrac{5}{2}\right] -\left[20 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-\dfrac{45}2}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}-3\sqrt{x}\,dx + \displaystyle\int^1_{-4}(x-3)\,dx$

$ =\greenD{-14} + \left(\greenD{-\dfrac{45}{2}}\right)$

$ = -\dfrac{73}{2}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^4_{-2}f(x)\,dx =  -\dfrac{73}{2}$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^2_{-2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^2_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$=  \displaystyle\int^2_{0}6x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}(3x^2-2x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^2_{0}6x\,dx
&=3x^2\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[3 \cdot(\blueD 2)^2 \right] - \left[3 \cdot(\maroonD{0})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[12\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{12}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\displaystyle\int^0_{-2}(3x^2-2x)\,dx
&=(x^3-x^2)\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[ (\blueD 0)^3 -  (\blueD 0)^2 \right] - \left[(\maroonD{-2})^3-(\maroonD{-2})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-12 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{12}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^2_{0}6x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}(3x^2-2x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{12} + \greenD{12}$

$ = 24$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^2_{-2}f(x)\,dx = 24$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$=  \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(3x^2-1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}(6x-1)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(3x^2-1)\,dx
&=(x^3-x)\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[ (\blueD 1)^3 - (\blueD1)\right] - \left[(\maroonD0)^3-(\maroonD{0})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{0}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align}  \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}(6x-1)\,dx
&=3x^2-x\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[3 \cdot(\blueD 0)^2-(\blueD0) \right] - \left[3 \cdot(\maroonD{-1})^2-(\maroonD{-1})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-4}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}(3x^2-1)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}(6x-1)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{0} + (\greenD{-4})$

$ = -4$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx = -4$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^4_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$=  \displaystyle\int^4_{1}(4-4x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}(6x^2-6)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}(4-4x)\,dx
&=4x-2x^2\Bigg|^\blueD4_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[4\cdot(\blueD4)-2\cdot (\blueD 4)^2 \right] - \left[4\cdot(\maroonD1)-2\cdot (\maroonD 1)^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-16\right] -\left[2 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-18}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}(6x^2-6)\,dx
&=(2x^3-6x)\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[ 2\cdot(\blueD 1)^3 -6\cdot(\blueD1)\right] - \left[2\cdot(\maroonD 0)^3 -6\cdot(\maroonD0)\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-4\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-4}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^4_{1}(4-4x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}(6x^2-6)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{-18} + (\greenD{-4})$

$ = -22$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^4_{0}f(x)\,dx = -22$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-4}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-4}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$=  \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}(4x^3+2)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-4}(3x+1)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}(4x^3+2)\,dx
&=(x^4+2x)\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[ (\blueD 0)^4+2\cdot(\blueD0) \right] - \left[ (\maroonD {-1})^4+2\cdot(\maroonD{-1}) \right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-1 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{1}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-4}(3x+1)\,dx
&=\left(\dfrac32x^2+x\right)\Bigg|^\blueD{-1}_{\maroonD{-4}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac32 (\blueD {-1})^2 +(\blueD {-1}) \right] - \left[\dfrac32 (\maroonD {-4})^2 +(\maroonD {-4})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac12\right] -\left[20 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-\dfrac{39}{2}}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^0_{-1}(4x^3+2)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-4}(3x+1)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{1} + \left(\greenD{-\dfrac{39}{2}}\right)$

$ = -\dfrac{37}{2}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^0_{-4}f(x)\,dx = -\dfrac{37}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
x^2-3x &   \text{for} ~~~~x\gt{2} \\
2x-6&  \text{for} ~~~~ x \leq2\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^3_{2}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{2}_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$=  \displaystyle\int^3_{2}(x^2-3x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{2}_{0}(2x-6)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^3_{2}(x^2-3x)\,dx
&=\left(\dfrac13x^3-\dfrac32x^2\right)\Bigg|^\blueD3_{\maroonD{2}} \\\\
&= \left[ \dfrac13\cdot(\blueD 3)^3 - \dfrac32\cdot(\blueD3)^2 \right] - \left[  \dfrac13\cdot(\maroonD 2)^3 - \dfrac32\cdot(\maroonD2)^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-\dfrac92\right] -\left[-\dfrac{10}{3} \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-\dfrac76}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{2}_{0}(2x-6)\,dx
&=(x^2-6x)\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[(\blueD 2)^2 -6\cdot(\blueD2)\right] - \left[(\maroonD{0})^2-6\cdot(\maroonD0)\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-8\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-8}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{2}(x^2-3x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{2}_{0}(2x-6)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{-\dfrac76} + (\greenD{-8})$

$ = -\dfrac{55}{6}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = -\dfrac{55}{6}$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{x} &   \text{for} ~~~~x\gt{1} \\
x&  \text{for} ~~~~ x \leq1\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^3_{1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\dfrac1x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}x\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\dfrac1x\,dx
&=\ln(x)\Bigg|^\blueD3_{\maroonD{1}} \\\\
&= \left[\ln(\blueD 3) \right] - \left[\ln (\maroonD{1})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\ln(3)\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\ln(3)}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}x\,dx
&=\dfrac12x^2\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12\cdot\left(\blueD{1} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ (\maroonD{0})^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac12\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac12}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^3_{1}\dfrac1x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{1}_{0}x\,dx$

$ = \greenD{\ln(3)} + \greenD{\dfrac12}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = \ln(3) +\dfrac12$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-2}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{0}4e^x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}(4-x)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}4e^x\,dx
&=4e^x\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[4e^\blueD1 \right] - \left[4e^\maroonD{0}\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[4e\right] -\left[4 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{4e-4}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}(4-x)\,dx
&=4x-\dfrac12x^2\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-2}} \\\\
&= \left[4\cdot(\blueD0)-\dfrac12\cdot\left(\blueD{0} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ 4\cdot(\maroonD{-2})-\dfrac12\cdot\left(\maroonD{-2}\right)^2\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[0\right] -\left[-10 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{10}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{0}4e^x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-2}(4-x)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{4e-4} + \greenD{10}$

$ = \greenD{4e+6}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-2}f(x)\,dx = 4e+6$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
x+4 &   \text{for} ~~~~x\gt{-1} \\
-\dfrac3x&  \text{for} ~~~~ x \leq-1\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-3}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(x+4)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}\left(-\dfrac3x\right)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(x+4)\,dx
&=\left(\dfrac12x^2+4x\right)\Bigg|^\blueD1_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12\left(\blueD{1} \right)^2 +4\cdot(\blueD1)\right] - \left[ \dfrac12\left(\maroonD{-1} \right)^2 +4\cdot(\maroonD{-1})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[\dfrac92\right] -\left[-\dfrac72 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{8}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}\left(-\dfrac3x\right)\,dx
&=-3\ln(|x|)\Bigg|^\blueD{-1}_{\maroonD{-3}} \\\\
&=-\left[-3\ln(|\blueD {-1}|) \right] - \left[-3\ln (|\maroonD{-3}|)\right]  \\\\
&=-\left[-3\ln(\blueD {1}) \right] - \left[-3\ln (\maroonD{3})\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-3\cdot0\right] -\left[-3\ln(3) \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{3\ln(3)}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^1_{-1}(x+4)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{-1}_{-3}\left(-\dfrac3x\right)\,dx$

$ =\greenD{8} + \greenD{3\ln(3)} $

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^3_{0}f(x)\,dx = 3\ln(3) +8$

##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^2_{-1}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^2_{0}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^2_{0}(2x-6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}\left(-6e^x\right)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^2_{0}(2x-6)\,dx
&=(x^2-6x)\Bigg|^\blueD2_{\maroonD{0}} \\\\
&= \left[\left(\blueD{2} \right)^2 - 6\cdot(\blueD2) \right] - \left[ \left(\maroonD{0} \right)^2 - 6\cdot(\maroonD0)\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-8\right] -\left[0 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-8}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}\left(-6e^x\right)\,dx
&=-6e^x\Bigg|^\blueD0_{\maroonD{-1}} \\\\
&= \left[-6e^{\blueD 0} \right] - \left[-6e^{\maroonD{-1}}\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-6\right] -\left[-6e^{-1} \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{-6+6e^{-1}}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^2_{0}(2x-6)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{0}_{-1}\left(-6e^x\right)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{-8} + (\greenD{-6+6e^{-1}})$ 

$ = -14+6e^{-1}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^2_{-1}f(x)\,dx = -14+6e^{-1}$

assessment_items.question_content:
$f(x) = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{5}{x} &   \text{for} ~~~~x\gt{5} \\
x-4&  \text{for} ~~~~ x \leq5\end{cases}$

**Evaluate the definite integral.**

$\displaystyle\int^6_{4}f(x)\,dx = $

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Splitting up the definite integral

Since we're working with a piecewise function, we need to split the definite integral up into two pieces:


$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^6_{4}f(x)\,dx$ 

$= \displaystyle\int^6_{5}f(x)\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{5}_{4}f(x)\,dx~~~~~~$ [[☃ explanation 1]]

$= \displaystyle\int^6_{5}\dfrac5x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{5}_{4}(x-4)\,dx ~~~~~~$

##Evaluating each piece

Next, let's evaluate each of these definite integrals one at a time.

The first definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^6_{5}\dfrac5x\,dx
&=5\ln(x)\Bigg|^\blueD6_{\maroonD{5}} \\\\
&= \left[5\ln(\blueD 6) \right] - \left[5\ln (\maroonD{5})\right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{5\ln\left(\dfrac65\right)}\end{align}$

The second definite integral:

$\begin{align} \displaystyle\int^{5}_{4}(x-4)\,dx
&=\dfrac12x^2-4x\Bigg|^\blueD5_{\maroonD{4}} \\\\
&= \left[\dfrac12\cdot\left(\blueD{5}  \right)^2 - 4\cdot(\blueD5) \right] - \left[ \dfrac12\cdot\left(\maroonD{4}  \right)^2 - 4\cdot(\maroonD4)\right]  \\\\
 &= \left[-\dfrac{15}2\right] -\left[-8 \right]  \\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac12}\end{align}$

## Putting the pieces together

Now let's add these two pieces together to find the answer:

$\phantom{=} \displaystyle\int^6_{5}\dfrac5x\,dx + \displaystyle\int^{5}_{4}(x-4)\,dx$

$ = \greenD{5\ln\left(\dfrac65\right)} + \greenD{\dfrac12}$

## The answer

$\displaystyle\int^6_{4}f(x)\,dx = 5\ln\left(\dfrac65\right) +\dfrac12$
